abstract: Computer-use agents hold the promise of assisting in a wide range of digital economic activities. However, current research has largely focused on short-horizon tasks over a limited set of software with limited economic value, such as basic e-commerce and OS-configuration tasks. A key reason is that creating environments for complex software requires significant time and human effort, and therefore does not scale. To address this, we introduce Gym-Anything, a framework for converting any software into an interactive computer-use environment. We frame environment creation itself as a multi-agent task: a coding agent writes setup scripts, downloads real-world data, and configures the software, while producing evidence of correct setup. An independent audit agent then verifies evidence for the environment setup against a quality checklist. Using a taxonomy of economically valuable occupations grounded in U.S. GDP data, we apply this pipeline to 200 software applications with broad occupational coverage. The result is CUA-World, a collection of over 10K long-horizon tasks spanning domains from medical science and astronomy to engineering and enterprise systems, each configured with realistic data along with train and test splits. CUA-World also includes CUA-World-Long, a challenging long-horizon benchmark with tasks often requiring over 500 steps, far exceeding existing benchmarks. Distilling successful trajectories from the training split into a 2B vision-language model outperforms models 2$\times$ its size. We also apply the same auditing principle at test time: a separate VLM reviews completed trajectories and provides feedback on what remains, improving Gemini-3-Flash on CUA-World-Long from 11.5% to 14.0%. We release all code, infrastructure, and benchmark data to facilitate future research in realistic computer-use agents.

citations.fullCitation: Tianbao Xie, Danyang Zhang, Jixuan Chen, Xiaochuan Li, Siheng Zhao, Ruisheng Cao, Toh Jing Hua, Zhoujun Cheng, Dongchan Shin, Fangyu Lei, Yitao Liu, Yiheng Xu, Shuyan Zhou, Silvio Savarese, Caiming Xiong, Victor Zhong, and Tao Yu. OSWorld: Benchmarking multimodal agents for open-ended tasks in real computer environments. In A. Globerson, L. Mackey, D. Belgrave, A. Fan, U. Paquet, J. Tomczak, and C. Zhang, editors, Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, volume 37, pages 52040–52094. Curran Associates, Inc., 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduced OSWorld, a key prior interactive benchmark for computer-use agents on desktop operating systems. Gym-Anything directly compares its CUA-World benchmark against OSWorld to demonstrate its significant improvements in scale, task diversity, economic coverage, and automated environment creation.
citations.title: OSWorld: Benchmarking multimodal agents for open-ended tasks in real computer environments.

citations.fullCitation: Shuyan Zhou, Frank F. Xu, Hao Zhu, Xuhui Zhou, Robert Lo, Abishek Sridhar, Xianyi Cheng, Tianyue Ou, Yonatan Bisk, Daniel Fried, Uri Alon, and Graham Neubig. WebArena: A realistic web environment for building autonomous agents. In The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations, 2024. ICLR 2024; arXiv:2307.13854.
citations.justification: WebArena is cited as a prominent example of existing interactive benchmarks that focus on a narrow set of web-based tasks. The Gym-Anything paper uses it as a point of contrast to highlight its own framework's ability to create environments from a much broader and more economically significant range of software beyond web browsers.
citations.title: WebArena: A realistic web environment for building autonomous agents.

citations.fullCitation: Xiang Deng, Yu Gu, Boyuan Zheng, Shijie Chen, Sam Stevens, Boshi Wang, Huan Sun, and Yu Su. Mind2Web: Towards a generalist agent for the web. In A. Oh, T. Naumann, A. Globerson, K. Saenko, M. Hardt, and S. Levine, editors, Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, volume 36, pages 28091–28114. Curran Associates, Inc., 2023.
citations.justification: This paper is referenced as a large-scale static dataset for web agents. The Gym-Anything paper contrasts its interactive, execution-based evaluation approach with the action-matching evaluation used in static datasets like Mind2Web, arguing that interactive environments provide a more faithful assessment of an agent's capabilities.
citations.title: Mind2Web: Towards a generalist agent for the web.

citations.fullCitation: Christopher Rawles, Sarah Clinckemaillie, Yifan Chang, Jonathan Waltz, Gabrielle Lau, Mary-beth Fair, Alice Li, William Bishop, Wei Li, Folawiyo Campbell-Ajala, Daniel Toyama, Robert Berry, Divya Tyamagundlu, Timothy Lillicrap, and Oriana Riva. AndroidWorld: A dynamic benchmarking environment for autonomous agents. In The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations, 2025. ICLR 2025; arXiv:2405.14573.
citations.justification: AndroidWorld is a key interactive benchmark for agents on the Android platform. It is used as a direct comparison point in Table 2 to demonstrate CUA-World's superior scale in software diversity and task count, and to show that Gym-Anything's framework can handle multiple operating systems, including mobile.
citations.title: AndroidWorld: A dynamic benchmarking environment for autonomous agents.

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Gym-Anything: Turn any Software into an Agent Environment" by Aggarwal, Neubig, and Welleck.

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research are Pranjal Aggarwal, Graham Neubig, and Sean Welleck, all affiliated with Carnegie Mellon University (CMU).

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The field of computer-use agents (CUAs), also known as graphical user interface (GUI) agents, aims to develop AI systems capable of interacting with software to automate and assist in digital tasks. Existing research in this area has primarily focused on short-horizon tasks, typically within a limited selection of software applications. Current benchmarks often involve consumer-grade applications or simplified web interfaces, such as changing desktop wallpapers or filling basic web forms. These tasks often require a small number of steps and may not adequately reflect the complexity and diversity of real-world professional work.

A significant challenge in advancing CUA research is the prohibitive cost and time investment required to create realistic, interactive environments for complex software. Each software application typically demands manual installation, configuration with domain-specific data, task design, and verification, often extending over weeks of expert human effort. This manual process severely limits the scalability of environment creation, leading to a gap between the capabilities tested in benchmarks and the demands of economically valuable occupations.

This work addresses this scalability bottleneck by introducing a framework for automated environment creation. It aims to broaden the scope of CUA research beyond existing limitations by enabling the generation of diverse, long-horizon tasks across a wide array of software applications, grounded in their economic relevance. The paper positions itself as a critical step towards developing agents capable of handling professional workflows that involve heterogeneous environments, requiring hundreds of steps and diverse software configured with specific data. This contrasts with prior approaches that either rely on static datasets (which may penalize valid alternative strategies in action-matching evaluations) or interactive benchmarks that, while providing execution-based evaluation, cover only a narrow slice of the software landscape due to manual environment construction.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a scalable framework for automatically constructing realistic, interactive computer-use environments across a wide range of economically valuable software applications. This overarching goal is driven by several key motivations:

*   **Bridging the Gap to Real-World Professional Work:** Digitally intensive occupations represent a substantial portion of the economy, collectively contributing trillions of dollars in GDP. However, current computer-use agents are largely unable to handle the complexities of real professional workflows, which are characterized by long-horizon tasks and interactions within heterogeneous software environments. The existing benchmarks, primarily focused on short-horizon tasks in a limited set of applications, do not adequately prepare or evaluate agents for such real-world demands.
*   **Addressing Unfaithful Evaluation and Limited Training Signal:** The narrow scope and short-horizon nature of existing benchmarks lead to two main consequences. First, evaluation becomes unfaithful; high scores on current benchmarks may not accurately reflect an agent's ability to operate software central to economic activity. Second, the training signal for agents is limited; short-horizon tasks over few applications may not provide the diverse and long-horizon trajectories necessary to train agents capable of complex, multi-step real-world work.
*   **Overcoming the Cost of Environment Creation:** The fundamental barrier to scaling CUA research is the substantial human effort and time required for creating realistic environments. This includes software installation, configuration with domain-appropriate and realistic data, and task verification, which can take weeks per application. The authors seek to automate this labor-intensive process to facilitate the creation of environments at a much larger scale.
*   **Grounding Software Selection in Economic Value:** Unlike previous benchmarks that often select software based on convenience or popularity, a key motivation is to prioritize software that drives significant economic activity. This ensures that the developed environments and tasks are relevant to the real economy and contribute to solving practical problems.
*   **Developing Robust and Granular Task Verification:** For long-horizon, complex tasks, a simple binary pass/fail verification is insufficient. The motivation is to develop a verification system that is both robust and granular, allowing for partial credit and distinguishing between genuine task failures and attempts to bypass intended workflows.

In summary, the authors are motivated to scale the creation of realistic, economically relevant, and complex CUA environments and tasks to accelerate the development of agents capable of performing valuable work in the digital economy.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology employed in "Gym-Anything" involves a multi-stage, multi-agent pipeline designed to automate the creation of interactive computer-use environments and the generation of diverse, long-horizon tasks.

**4.1. The Gym-Anything Framework**
The core of this work is the Gym-Anything library, which standardizes environment creation. It abstracts each environment into a minimal specification: three sequential setup scripts (`install`, `configure`, `task_setup`) and a declarative configuration file (`env.json`). This structure separates general software installation from data configuration and task-specific setup, enabling modularity and reuse. The library handles the underlying complexities of container orchestration, display forwarding, and checkpoint management across various operating systems (Linux, Windows, Android) and compute backends (e.g., Docker, Apptainer). It also exposes a standard Gymnasium-style API for agent interaction, providing unified observation (e.g., screenshots) and action spaces (e.g., keyboard and mouse inputs).

**4.2. GDP-Grounded Software Selection**
To ensure economic relevance, the authors developed a systematic procedure for selecting software:
1.  **GDP per Occupation Estimation:** Using O\*NET data for occupations and Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) / Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) data for employment, wages, and national accounts, they estimate the GDP attributed to each of approximately 900 U.S. occupations.
2.  **Software Discovery per Occupation:** An LLM with web-search capabilities is used to identify relevant software categories and enumerate specific software applications (around 16,600 across 1,400 categories) used within each occupation.
3.  **GDP Attribution to Software:** Each occupation's GDP is decomposed and attributed to individual software applications based on the fraction of computer-involved work and the software's share within its category, estimated by an LLM with web-search access.
4.  **Filtering for Sandboxable Software:** Software applications are filtered based on criteria such as being self-hostable, having a free tier, possessing a GUI, and not requiring specialized, non-simulatable hardware. If a high-GDP software is not sandboxable, the closest sandboxable alternative from the same category is substituted.
5.  **Tiered Selection:** From approximately 3,400 sandboxable candidates, 200 software applications are selected across five tiers to balance economic importance (highest-GDP software), strategic coverage (healthcare, STEM domains), diversity (all 22 Standard Occupational Classification groups), and niche/category filling.

**4.3. Multi-Agent Environment Creation (Creation-Audit Loop)**
To scale environment construction, the process is framed as a multi-agent task:
*   **Creation Agent (`Agent_C`):** An instance of Claude Opus 4.5/4.6 (via Claude Code) equipped with bash, Python, and computer-use tools. `Agent_C` researches software, finds and downloads real-world data, implements setup scripts, launches the environment, performs visual verification (screenshots, logs), and iteratively debugs. Crucially, it is required to produce "evidence" of correct setup.
*   **Audit Agent (`Agent_audit`):** An independent Claude Opus agent that acts adversarially. It evaluates the evidence produced by `Agent_C` against a predefined quality checklist, analyzing screenshots, execution logs, and configuration files to verify the environment's state. It provides feedback on issues, which `Agent_C` uses for correction. This separation is intended to mitigate self-confirmation bias.
*   **Shared Memory (`M`):** `Agent_C` maintains `M` to store learnings from each environment creation attempt, categorized as software-specific (`M_soft`) or general (`M_general`). A `Memory Summarization Agent` (`Agent_summ`) periodically condenses `M_soft` into `M_general` to manage context length and facilitate faster learning for new environments. This iterative loop continues for multiple rounds until the environment meets quality standards.

**4.4. Scaling Tasks (Propose-and-Amplify Strategy)**
Generating diverse and realistic tasks for these environments is also automated:
*   **Propose (Seed Tasks):** An expensive, agentic model (Claude Opus 4.5/4.6) creates a small set of high-quality, difficult "seed tasks" for each software, ensuring realism, difficulty, and functional diversity by actively running the software and interacting via the GUI.
*   **Amplify (Additional Tasks):** A cheaper, non-agentic language model (Gemini 3 Pro) uses these seed tasks as in-context examples to generate a larger volume of additional tasks. To maintain diversity, tasks are generated sequentially, with previous instructions provided as context, followed by semantic similarity filtering.
*   **Automated Filtering:** Each generated task is launched, and a Vision-Language Model (VLM) checks if the initial state matches the task description, filtering out invalid tasks.

**4.5. Task Verification**
A robust verification system is critical for long-horizon tasks:
*   **Privileged Information (`I`):** Ground-truth data is automatically extracted from the environment's setup scripts (e.g., correct tumor location from a dataset, expected account balances). This information is used by the verifier but is not available to the agent solving the task.
*   **Checklist-based VLM Verifier:** A VLM uses `I` and the task instruction to generate a granular checklist (`C`) of weighted subtasks. The VLM then reviews the agent's trajectory (`τ`) to determine if each subtask was completed, providing a score that allows for partial credit.
*   **Integrity Checklist (`C_int`):** A separate set of VLM checks (`C_int`) ensures the agent did not bypass the intended workflow (e.g., directly editing files instead of using the GUI, fabricating output). Failing any integrity check results in a zero score for the task.

**4.6. Construction of CUA-World and CUA-World-Long**
Applying these methodologies resulted in CUA-World, a collection of over 10,000 tasks across 200 software applications. The dataset is divided into train and test splits, with a contamination filtering process to prevent data leakage (using an LLM to score task similarity and grouping highly similar tasks into the same split). CUA-World-Long is a specialized benchmark of 200 extremely long-horizon tasks (one per software), designed by an AI system that analyzes agent failure patterns on existing tasks to create more challenging scenarios.

**4.7. Experimental Setup**
*   **Training:** Successful trajectories from CUA-World-Train (generated by a strong teacher model, Kimi-K 2.5) are distilled into a smaller student model (Qwen3-VL-2B).
*   **Test-Time Auditing (TTA):** An independent VLM reviews an agent's completed trajectory and provides feedback on uncompleted subtasks, prompting the main agent to continue, aiming to address premature stopping common in long-horizon tasks.
*   **Evaluation:** Agents are evaluated on CUA-World-Test and CUA-World-Long using the checklist-based VLM verifier, reporting Average Score and Pass Rate, with specific step and cost caps.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents several findings regarding the CUA-World benchmark, agent performance, and the efficacy of the proposed methodologies.

**5.1. CUA-World Characteristics and Scale**
CUA-World comprises over 10,000 long-horizon tasks across 200 software applications, grounded in U.S. GDP data. It covers all 22 major occupation groups, supports three operating systems (Linux, Windows, Android), and includes train/test splits. CUA-World is characterized as the first collection to simultaneously offer interactive environments at this scale, support long-horizon evaluation, provide broad occupational coverage, facilitate automated environment creation, and include a training split. The CUA-World-Long benchmark consists of 200 tasks (one per software), often requiring over 500 steps, which is noted as exceeding existing benchmarks in complexity.

**5.2. Agent Performance on CUA-World-Test and CUA-World-Long**
*   **CUA-World-Test:** Frontier models like Gemini 3 Flash achieve an average score of 50.1 and a 22.6% pass rate, while Kimi-K 2.5 achieves 37.1 average score and 12.8% pass rate. Smaller models, such as Qwen3-VL-2B, perform poorly (1.6% pass rate). Distillation of successful trajectories from the training split into a 2B vision-language model boosts its pass rate from 1.6% to 4.4%, enabling it to outperform a 4B model (3.9% pass rate), suggesting the utility of CUA-World-Train as a supervision signal.
*   **CUA-World-Long:** This benchmark proves challenging for even frontier models. Gemini 3 Flash achieves a 7.5% pass rate and 36.2 average score with a $5 cost cap (max 500 steps). When the cost cap is removed and steps are increased to 2000, Gemini 3 Flash's pass rate rises to 11.5%, and GPT-5.4 achieves 27.5% pass rate. This indicates that task difficulty on CUA-World-Long often requires extensive steps, highlighting a persistent challenge for current models in handling long-horizon tasks efficiently.

**5.3. Scaling Behavior and Generalization**
*   **Training Data Scaling:** The performance of the distilled 2B model on CUA-World-Test consistently improves with increased training data, showing a roughly log-linear trend of approximately a 3.5-point increase in average score for doubling the data, whether by increasing the number of software applications or the fraction of tasks.
*   **Generalization to Unseen Software:** While distillation improves performance on both seen (IID) and unseen (OOD) software, the gains are more pronounced on IID software (65-87% of potential gain recovered) compared to OOD software (22-27% recovered). This suggests that while some generalization occurs, training on a diverse set of software, including software-specific training, remains important for broad capabilities.

**5.4. Test-Time Auditing (TTA)**
The application of an independent VLM audit agent at test time improves Gemini 3 Flash's pass rate on CUA-World-Long from 11.5% to 14.0%. This indicates that TTA can help agents overcome common failure modes, such as prematurely terminating tasks, by providing feedback on uncompleted subtasks.

**5.5. Trajectory Behavioral Analysis**
An analysis of Gemini 3 Flash trajectories on CUA-World reveals behavioral patterns:
*   **Failure Modes:** Failed trajectories exhibit significantly more "retry loops" (78% of steps) compared to passed ones (39%). "Access blockers" also account for a larger fraction of steps in failed trajectories (23% vs 4%).
*   **Success Correlates:** "Verification checks" are present in a higher proportion of passed trajectories (91%) than failed ones (70%), suggesting a correlation between self-verification and task success. "UI exploration" is high in both, indicating significant effort in navigation.

**5.6. Performance by Software Category**
*   **Visual Complexity:** Smaller models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-2B) show a steep decline in pass rate as visual complexity increases (3.2% on low-complexity to 0.0% on high-complexity), a disparity that persists even after distillation. Larger models maintain more consistent performance across complexity levels.
*   **Domain Knowledge:** All models show a downward trend in performance from general to specialized software, with smaller models exhibiting a steeper decline.

**5.7. Verifier Robustness and Ablations**
*   **VLM Verifier:** The checklist-based VLM verifier achieves 93.3% agreement with human annotations, outperforming a direct VLM verifier (81.7%) and programmatic verifiers (43.3%). Integrity checks catch workflow bypasses in a small but relevant fraction of cases (15 true positives out of 21 flagged high-scoring runs).
*   **Distillation Ablations:** It was found that the strongest teacher model (Opus 4.5) did not necessarily produce the strongest student model (Kimi-K 2.5 produced better students despite a lower teacher score), suggesting factors beyond raw performance, such as reasoning chains, may be relevant. Training trajectory length had varied effects depending on the task's complexity.
*   **Propose-and-Amplify:** The "propose" (seed task) step significantly improved the quality of amplified tasks, leading to an 88.9% setup success rate compared to 55.2% without seeds. Seeds grounded the tasks in professional workflows and guided realistic data preparation.
*   **Cross-Model Auditing:** Using an independent audit model caught more issues (an average of 2.1 additional issues per environment) than self-auditing, indicating that separating the creation and audit agents reduces self-confirmation bias.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research introduces Gym-Anything, a framework designed to address a fundamental challenge in the development of computer-use agents: the scalable and automated creation of realistic, interactive software environments. The work's significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

*   **Enabling Scalable Environment Creation:** By framing environment creation as a multi-agent task and standardizing the specification of environments, Gym-Anything offers a methodology to convert virtually any software into an interactive agent environment. This dramatically reduces the manual effort and time traditionally required, removing a significant bottleneck for CUA research.
*   **Expanding Research Scope and Realism:** The resulting CUA-World collection, with over 10,000 tasks across 200 software applications, spans diverse occupational domains (all 22 SOC groups) and operating systems. This provides a more realistic and economically relevant benchmark and training ground for agents, moving beyond short-horizon tasks in limited applications.
*   **Addressing Long-Horizon Task Challenges:** The introduction of CUA-World-Long, a benchmark featuring tasks often requiring hundreds of steps, provides a dedicated challenge for agents to develop and demonstrate long-term planning and execution capabilities, which are crucial for real-world professional workflows.
*   **Improving Agent Training and Evaluation:** The work demonstrates the utility of distilling trajectories from a powerful teacher model into smaller student models, showing that CUA-World can provide a useful supervision signal. The proposed checklist-based VLM verification, augmented with privileged information, offers a granular and robust method for evaluating agent performance, including partial credit and integrity checks against workflow bypasses. The Test-Time Auditing mechanism further enhances agent performance by providing feedback on uncompleted tasks, aiding in overcoming premature termination.
*   **Facilitating Future Research:** The release of all code, infrastructure, and benchmark data aims to provide a foundation for subsequent work in computer-use agents. This open-source contribution can foster further research in developing more capable agents, robust verifiers, and broader coverage of software relevant to the digital economy.
*   **Potential for Economic Automation:** By aligning environment and task design with economically valuable occupations and real-world software, this research contributes to the long-term goal of developing agents that can meaningfully automate or assist in digitally intensive professional tasks, potentially impacting productivity and economic activity.

Overall, Gym-Anything and CUA-World represent a step towards scaling the creation and evaluation of computer-use agents on tasks that are more representative of real-world complexity and economic value.
overview:
The development of computer-use agents (CUAs)—AI systems capable of navigating graphical user interfaces (GUIs) to perform digital tasks—is a major focus in artificial intelligence. However, current research is often limited by the scope of existing benchmarks. Most current environments focus on short-horizon tasks in a small number of consumer applications, such as changing a desktop background or filling out a simple web form. These tasks do not reflect the complexity of professional software used in the global economy, where workflows may involve hundreds of steps and specialized domain knowledge.

The primary barrier to progress is the high cost of environment creation. Manually installing software, configuring it with realistic data, and designing verifiable tasks can take weeks of expert human effort per application. Gym-Anything addresses this scalability bottleneck by introducing a framework for the automated creation of interactive environments and tasks across a wide array of software, grounded in their economic relevance.

## The Gym-Anything Framework

At the core of this work is the Gym-Anything library, which standardizes the process of turning software into an interactive agent environment. To ensure modularity and scalability, the framework abstracts environment creation into three sequential setup scripts: $install$, $configure$, and $task\_setup$. These are accompanied by a declarative configuration file, $env.json$.

The $install$ script handles the base installation of the software. The $configure$ script populates the environment with domain-specific, realistic data (e.g., medical records for a healthcare app or financial logs for accounting software). Finally, the $task\_setup$ script prepares the environment for a specific goal. This separation allows researchers to reuse the installation and configuration logic across thousands of different tasks.

Gym-Anything provides a unified Gymnasium-style API that handles complex backend operations across multiple operating systems, including Linux, Windows, and Android. It manages container orchestration (using Docker or Apptainer), display forwarding, and checkpointing. Agents interact with these environments through a standard interface: they receive visual observations (screenshots) and execute actions via keyboard and mouse inputs.

## Grounding Software Selection in Economic Value

To ensure that the research contributes to solving real-world problems, the authors developed a systematic approach to selecting software based on its contribution to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Rather than choosing software based on popularity or ease of automation, they identified applications that drive significant economic activity.

The selection process involves several steps:
1.  **GDP Estimation:** Using data from O*NET and the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the authors estimated the GDP attributed to approximately 900 U.S. occupations.
2.  **Software Discovery:** For each occupation, large language models (LLMs) with web-search capabilities identified relevant software categories and specific applications.
3.  **GDP Attribution:** The GDP of an occupation was decomposed and attributed to specific software based on the fraction of computer-based work and the software's market share within its category.
4.  **Filtering:** The software was filtered to ensure it could be "sandboxable"—meaning it is self-hostable, has a GUI, and does not require specialized hardware that cannot be simulated.

This resulted in a tiered selection of 200 software applications covering all 22 major U.S. Standard Occupational Classification groups, including high-GDP software in healthcare, STEM, and finance.

## The Multi-Agent Creation Pipeline

The most labor-intensive part of building environments—writing the setup scripts and verifying the installation—is automated through a multi-agent "Creation-Audit" loop. This process uses two distinct agents to reduce bias and ensure quality.

The Creation Agent, $Agent_C$, is responsible for researching the software, downloading data, implementing the setup scripts, and launching the environment. Crucially, $Agent_C$ is tasked with providing "evidence" of a successful setup, such as specific execution logs or screenshots of the GUI in its configured state.

The Audit Agent, $Agent_{audit}$, acts as an independent reviewer. It evaluates the evidence produced by $Agent_C$ against a predefined quality checklist. If the environment fails the audit, $Agent_{audit}$ provides feedback, and $Agent_C$ attempts to fix the issues. This adversarial relationship helps prevent "self-confirmation bias," where a single agent might overlook its own mistakes.

To improve efficiency over time, the system uses a shared memory $M$. $Agent_C$ stores specific learnings in $M_{soft}$ (software-specific) and $M_{general}$ (general principles). A Memory Summarization Agent, $Agent_{summ}$, periodically condenses these logs to help the system learn general rules for environment creation, such as how to handle common installation errors or how to bypass specific GUI configuration prompts.

## Scaling Task Generation: Propose-and-Amplify

Once an environment is established, it needs diverse and realistic tasks. The researchers use a two-step "Propose-and-Amplify" strategy to generate these tasks at scale.

In the **Propose** phase, a high-capability agentive model explores the software GUI to create a small set of "seed tasks." These tasks are designed to be difficult and representative of professional use cases. Because the agent actually interacts with the software, the tasks are grounded in functional reality.

In the **Amplify** phase, a more cost-effective, non-agentive model uses these seed tasks as in-context examples to generate thousands of additional tasks. To maintain diversity, the model generates tasks sequentially while considering previously generated instructions. Finally, an automated filtering step uses a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to verify that the initial state of the software actually matches the task description.

## Robust and Granular Task Verification

Evaluating an agent on a 500-step task is more difficult than evaluating it on a 5-step task. A simple success/failure binary is often insufficient for long-horizon work. Gym-Anything implements a robust verification system based on three components:

1.  **Privileged Information ($I$):** This consists of ground-truth data extracted directly from the environment setup scripts (e.g., a specific database entry or a file hidden in a directory). This information is used by the verifier but remains hidden from the agent performing the task.
2.  **Checklist-based VLM Verifier:** A VLM uses the task instructions and $I$ to generate a checklist $C$. It then reviews the agent's trajectory $\tau$ and assigns partial credit based on how many sub-goals were completed. This approach achieved a 93.3% agreement rate with human annotators.
3.  **Integrity Checklist ($C_{int}$):** To prevent agents from "cheating" by bypassing the GUI (e.g., using a terminal to move a file instead of using the software's internal menu), an integrity check ensures the agent followed the intended workflow. If an agent fails $C_{int}$, it receives a zero score regardless of whether the final goal was achieved.

## CUA-World Benchmarks and Agent Performance

The application of this framework resulted in two major benchmarks: **CUA-World** (over 10,000 tasks across 200 apps) and **CUA-World-Long** (200 extremely long-horizon tasks).

The performance of state-of-the-art models on these benchmarks reveals the current limitations of AI agents. On CUA-World-Test, frontier models like Gemini 3 Flash achieve a pass rate of approximately 22.6%. On the more challenging CUA-World-Long, where tasks can require over 500 steps, the pass rate drops significantly to 7.5% under standard constraints.

The research also explored "Distillation," where successful trajectories from a strong teacher model were used to train a smaller 2B parameter student model. This training led to a significant improvement in the student's performance, allowing it to outperform models twice its size. This suggests that the CUA-World dataset provides a valuable training signal for improving the reasoning and execution capabilities of smaller, more efficient agents.

## Insights and Behavioral Analysis

The study provides several insights into how agents succeed or fail in complex environments:
*   **Retry Loops:** Failed trajectories are characterized by high levels of "retry loops," where an agent repeats the same unsuccessful action multiple times.
*   **Self-Verification:** Successful agents are more likely to perform "verification checks" (e.g., checking if a file saved correctly) during their workflow.
*   **Test-Time Auditing (TTA):** The researchers found that using an independent VLM to audit the agent's progress during execution—and providing feedback if a sub-task was missed—improved pass rates. This helps mitigate "premature stopping," a common issue where agents believe they have finished a task when they have actually missed a crucial step.

## Significance of the Work

Gym-Anything provides the first scalable pipeline for creating interactive, economically grounded environments for computer-use agents. By automating the environment setup and task generation process, it allows the research community to move beyond simplified web tasks and toward the complex, long-horizon workflows that define professional digital work. The release of the CUA-World benchmark provides a rigorous testing ground for the next generation of AI agents, highlighting the need for better planning, more robust self-verification, and improved visual reasoning in complex software interfaces.

keyInsights: Automating interactive environment creation via a multi-agent pipeline, enhanced by adversarial auditing, is key to scaling computer-use agent research beyond existing limitations.
Grounding software and task selection in economic value (GDP attribution) ensures the relevance and impact of benchmarks for real-world professional automation.

Granular, VLM-based task verification, leveraging privileged information and integrity checks, is essential for accurately evaluating agent performance on complex, long-horizon tasks and preventing workflow bypasses.
originalProblem: Computer-use agent (CUA) research focused on short-horizon tasks within a limited selection of software, failing to reflect complex, real-world professional workflows.
Prohibitive cost and time investment were required for manual creation of realistic, interactive environments and diverse tasks for complex software applications.
Existing benchmarks led to unfaithful agent evaluation and provided limited training signals for long-horizon, multi-step tasks in heterogeneous environments.
results: Created CUA-World, a benchmark comprising over 10,000 long-horizon tasks across 200 economically relevant software applications, covering all 22 major U.S. occupation groups.

Frontier models achieved moderate pass rates (e.g., Gemini 3 Flash: 22.6% on CUA-World-Test, 11.5% on CUA-World-Long with extended steps), indicating substantial room for improvement in handling complex tasks.

Distilling successful trajectories from CUA-World-Train into a 2B vision-language model boosted its pass rate from 1.6% to 4.4%, demonstrating the utility of the dataset for agent supervision; Test-Time Auditing further improved Gemini 3 Flash's pass rate on CUA-World-Long from 11.5% to 14.0%.
solution: Developed Gym-Anything, a framework standardizing environment creation with setup scripts and a declarative configuration, handling container orchestration and providing a Gymnasium-style API.
Implemented a multi-agent creation-audit loop, where a Creation Agent builds environments and an adversarial Audit Agent verifies setup, significantly automating the process.

Employed a propose-and-amplify strategy for task generation, creating seed tasks with an expensive agent and amplifying them with a cheaper model, followed by VLM-based filtering and a robust checklist verifier with integrity checks.
summary.summary: Carnegie Mellon University researchers introduced Gym-Anything, a framework that automates the creation of interactive software environments and over 10,000 long-horizon tasks for computer-use agents. This system enables the scalable development of benchmarks like CUA-World, which spans 200 economically relevant software applications and supports evaluation across diverse occupational domains.

title: Gym-Anything: Turn any Software into an Agent Environment
